title: Your First Cloud Deployment: Launch a Web Server Instance
description: Walk through a minimal OpenStack deployment: authenticate with Keystone, create a network with Neutron, launch a Nova instance, attach a Cinder volume, and verify using a runbook procedure.
prerequisites: Access to an OpenStack environment (devstack or lab)

steps.instruction: Authenticate with Keystone: run `openstack token issue` and examine the token fields. Identify the catalog field listing service endpoints. Which endpoint type (public/internal/admin) should a regular user application use?
steps.expectedOutcome: A scoped token is issued with an expiry time (~1 hour). The catalog shows service entries for nova, neutron, cinder, glance, swift, heat, and horizon. Public endpoints are used by external applications; internal by services communicating within the cloud.
steps.hint: The service catalog is embedded in the token body. `openstack catalog list` shows the parsed catalog.

steps.instruction: Create a private network and subnet: `openstack network create lab-net` then `openstack subnet create --network lab-net --subnet-range 10.0.0.0/24 lab-subnet`. Create a router and attach the subnet to it.
steps.expectedOutcome: A network named lab-net exists in your project. The subnet lab-subnet (10.0.0.0/24) is attached to a router that connects to the external provider network. `openstack network list` shows lab-net.
steps.hint: The router needs a gateway set to the external network: `openstack router set --external-gateway public lab-router`

steps.instruction: Create a security group allowing SSH: `openstack security group create web-sg` then add a rule for TCP port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0. Note the default-deny posture -- without this rule, no inbound traffic reaches instances.
steps.expectedOutcome: The web-sg security group exists with one ingress rule: TCP port 22, CIDR 0.0.0.0/0. All other inbound traffic remains blocked. This is the minimum security group for SSH access.
steps.hint: Also add ICMP allow to enable ping: `openstack security group rule create --protocol icmp web-sg`

steps.instruction: Launch a Nova instance: `openstack server create --flavor m1.small --image cirros --network lab-net --security-group web-sg my-server`. Wait for BUILD to transition to ACTIVE. Check `openstack server show my-server`.
steps.expectedOutcome: The instance transitions from BUILD -> ACTIVE in 30-60 seconds. `openstack server show` displays the private IP on lab-net, flavor details (vCPUs/RAM/disk), hypervisor, and creation timestamp.
steps.hint: If status stays in BUILD, check `openstack server event list my-server` for error events

steps.instruction: Create a Cinder block volume and attach it: `openstack volume create --size 1 my-vol` then `openstack server add volume my-server my-vol`. Inside the instance, find the new device at /dev/vdb and format it with mkfs.ext4.
steps.expectedOutcome: The volume appears as /dev/vdb inside the instance. After mkfs.ext4 and mount, it provides 1GB of persistent block storage that survives instance reboots (unlike the ephemeral root disk).
steps.hint: Block storage persists independently -- you can detach and reattach to a different instance

steps.instruction: Write a 3-step runbook for this deployment: (1) token issue + catalog verify, (2) network create + subnet attach, (3) instance launch + health check. Assign a VerificationMethod to each step (test, inspection, or demonstration).
steps.expectedOutcome: A structured runbook with 3 ProcedureSteps. Step 1: test (run token issue, observe output). Step 2: inspection (check network list, verify subnet entry). Step 3: demonstration (show instance ACTIVE status to reviewer). This matches NASA TAID verification assignment before ORR.
steps.hint: Inspection verifies without executing -- reviewing `openstack network list` output qualifies as inspection
